CURRENT AFFAIRS,

Owing to the combined rush of literary matter and
advertisements, the LEADER of this week is enlarged to
twenty-eight pages. We must apologise to many con-
tributors whose articles are held over notwithstanding ;
and to many correspondents whose valued information
is not dealt with owing to lack of space.

The Freeman, of Monday, has an editorial on ‘bhc
manifestations of Orange blackguardism at Bushmills,
as a consequence of the defeat of Mr. Moore, K.C.
The poor old Freeman, in its best toothless old woman
form, remarks: —* It 18 curious to remember that these
ignorant, bigoted, brutalised corner-boys haye been the
pampered pets of successive Governments in Ireland.
Legislation is shaped to suit their views, and even at
the present moment University Education is denied to
the great majority of the people of Ireland confessedly
because Orange bigotry objects.”’ The italics are ours.

Let us add that there is nothing curious about it at all.
It is only too plain. IHow can we blame ‘‘successive
Governments in Ireland ” for pandering to the Orange
fanatics when fthe Nationalists, as a whole, are too
tame, too fearful of heing called bigots by Rathmines
bogus lodgers and others, to stand up to the Orange-
men and other branches of ascendancy? Whilst the
Freeman thinks it curious that successive Governments
pampered ‘‘ these ignorant, bigoted, brutalised corner-
boys,”” we have Mr. Willic Redmond patting the bigot
Orangeman Crawford on the back as ‘“ a liberal-minded
Protestant,’”” and talking about ‘‘the day is soon coming
when, with God’s help, we will all be united for free-
dom and fair play in Ireland.” It is a pity Mr. Willie
Redmond is not earning, or seeking to earn, his living
in the Provincial Bank, the Belfast Corporation, or the
Portadown Urban Council.

All this raimeis about Orange and Green is only to be
paralleled by a corresponding school of opinion in Eng-
land which is ever and ever looking forward to the day:
when the people of Ireland will throw in their lot as
‘“loyal ”’ Britishers with the so-called ‘“ United King-
dom.” Even the Freeman, and even Mr. Willie Red-
mond, can see the absurdity of those simple English
people who dream of a British Ireland, notwithstanding
that they go on hoping for an Irish Orangeshire. How-
ever, the English believers in a happy British Ireland
are more sensible, for, notwithstanding their belief,
they keep on coercing and fighting the Irish; our ever-
lasting hopefuls are green.

The lot of people with pretensions to consistency who
take up untenable positions is like that of a policeman on
duty—not a happy one. During the discussion on the
clection of a Lord Mayor for Dublin, a gentleman who
believes it treason to Ireland to send M.P.’s to West-
minster, said, as reported :—“ He had not any faith,
from the national standpoint, in Parliamentary agita-
tion.  He looked upon Mr. Nannetti as a brilliant
leader in the trades union movement, and the Labour
representatives in Parliament would next year need
every man they had to carry their remedial measures.”
This is sailing in water-tight compartments with a ven-
geance. As a ““ Nationalist,’’ it is treason for Mr. Nan-
nebti to compromise Treland’s claims by going to West-
minster; as a Labour advocate it is desirable that M.
Nannetiti should compromise Ireland’s claims, and take
the oath of allegiance, as ‘‘ the Labour representatives
in Parliament would next year need every man they
had to carry their remedial measures.”” This is a de-
lightful arrangement—If one goes to Parliament he gets
a “ wipe’’ of Wolfe Tone across the right cheek; if he
abstains he gets a blow from the Cause of Labour on
the lefti!

There was a pretty little comedy enacted at the very
*“ exclusive *’ Rathmines Urban Council last week. This
Council, as our readers know, is a bye-word amongst
those who believe in Tolerance, Justice, and Fair Play.
One of the few Catholic members of this two-ward and
carefully rigged Council is Mr. M‘Bride. He is Vice-
Chairman! So long as the bigots keep a tight hold on
the paid posts they don’t mind giving an odd honorary
job such as that of Vice-Chairman to a helpless “ Idola-
tor.”” A Mr, Hewson moved that Mr. M‘Bride be re- -
elected Vice-Chairman. According to a newspaper re-
port Mr. Hewson said—" He did so with all the greater
pleasure bocause Mr. M‘Bride and he differed on cer-
tain points which he would not mention, but he was a
most admirable Vice-Chairman, and the motion would
be passed by all who desired to have equality and no
supremacy.”’  The italics are owrs. This is a fine

sample of Sourface tactics—tactics that so often throw
dust in the cyes of our green people. They rig for
themselves practically all the power, and nearly all the
better class paid posts are confined to those of the
Ppropar “colour,” and then they give a barren honorary
position to an ““ Idolator,” and one of their number
pats che rather exclusive assembly on the back for this
act of grace. The “Idolator,””” who we think would
have shown spirit by refusing the empty honour, duly
“returned thanks!”

In what way does¥he Dublin Chamber of Commerco
justify its existence? Has it done anything for the in-
dustrial development of Dublin or of Ireland? Our
versatile friend, Mr. William Field, M.P., said some
pertivant things at the annual meeting. The worst of
it is that Mr. Field is so much a rag on every bush that
his views, even when they are correct, carry little
weight. Mr. Field said:—“If it were not heresy to
Le would add that the Chamber was as dead as
ral.”  And again:—The real wealth of the
was the amount of produce that came out of it.
had yet to learn that the Dublin Chamber of
Commerce had made any effort to assist Irish industries.
In his opinion the Dublin Industries Development As-
sociation was superseding them, and he hoped that the
time would come when the Chamber of Commerce would
waken up to its duties and take part in the National
reviva! at home. The canal and waterways should he
developed.” These are sensible remarks of which, how-

ever. it would be too much to expect that those who
compose the Dublin Chamber of Commerce will take any
heed.

We see by a press report of a recent meeting of the
Cork Industrial Development Association that a “ Dub-
lin geutleman ”’ wrote for the name of an Irish manu-
facturzr of razors. We do not read that any reply was
given. One ncver knows, of course, under what big

Dark Brother within the four seas of Ireland now is his
time to let us know of his existence and we will give him
a cheap advertisement with pleasure.

FEBRUARY 3, 1906.

Over a hundred years ago—to be precise in 1798—
Father O’Neill was flogged through the streets of
Youghal and hanged by some sourfaces of that time.
There was a controversy about the matbter some time
back, and Mr. Edmund Lee, having distinguished him-
self in this controversy, was subsequently elected vice-
chairman of the local U.D.C.. Mr. Lec is now Hoxf;
Sec. of a proposed testimonial to a local bank manager
The manager of the branch of the Bank of I-ah-land at
Youghal is a Mr. Brabazon—a non-mere Trishman as
his name implies. He has “laboured ’’ in Youghal—
for a consideration—during three or four years, f‘"f!
now that he is about to be passed on “on promotion
to another place, somo people, wishful, perhaps, tor
verify thoe saying that it never rains but it pours, are
desirous of adding to the torrent of Mr. Brabazon’s joy
by tapping the generosity of Youghal. In plain words,
Mr. Brabazon having got “a rise,”” several people are
busy in sending round  the hat ”’ for him. Mr. Lee is
Hon. Sec. of this “spirited ”’ enterprise. Would not
Mr. Leo and more like him be better occupied in enquir-
ing into the status of their fellow ‘‘ Idolators’’ in the
Bank of I-ah-land than in inflicting upon struggling
Youghal a fresh financial burden in the shape of a tes-
timonial to a gentleman who is not only well proylde‘c’f
for, but is shifted, as we understand, ““ on promopop.
If the people of Youghal have money so flush it is a
wonder we have never heard of a testimonial for the
benefit of the admirable Youghal metal work industry.
Oh, no! testimonials are not for people who attempt
useful and constructive work; but the pockets of our
green people are supposed to be open as the day to bank
clerks and bobbies on the move!

We do not know whether or not Mr. Michael Davitt
will altogether appreciate the clap on the back from the
editor of a bitterly sectarian paper and likewise *“ Grand
Master ”’ Jof the  Independent. Orangemen.”” The
“ Grand Master,”” in the course of a recent harangue,
said : —“ Regarding the Education question, he stood by
the side of Michael Davitt. (Cheers, groans and hisses. )
Had those who were hissing Mr. Davitt’s name read his
letter  If they had, they must acknowledge as Protes-
tants that it was a letter to which they weuld willingly
subseribo their names.”

The annual general meeting of “ The National >’ Bank
was held in Zondon! In the course of the cosmopolitan
portion of his remarks, Mr. H. F. Slattery, the chair-
man, said:—“Tt is true that as against the foregoing:
we are fortunately able to congratulate ourselves upon
the Entente Cordiale established between this country
and France, due no less to the wisdom and unfailing’
tact of his Majesty the King than to the statesmanship
and able diplomatic action of Lord Lansdowne. The
Entente has happily taken deep root in the minds of the
people of both countries, and must tend to induce closer
business relations and to give a fresh impetus to trade.’”
]\’Vc thought the “National ”” Bank posed as an Ivish
bank.

Next Sunday the completion of another year of useful
temperance work will bo marked by the annual meeting-
of the Father Mathew Memorial Hall, Church Street.
The chair will be taken at 3 p.m. by His Grace the
Archbishop of Dublin, and the speakers will also in-
clude several other able and popular temperance advo-
cates. We have no doubt that the attendance of the:
public will be worthy of the occasion.

We do not grudge ./f Fowx, the libeller of Canon
Mc'[l{eruey, its little shout of jubilation over the South
Dublin  Election.  A2f badly wanted a tonic. Of
course, the gloater over the one-time proposed reduction
of the number of Irish seats devoted its first leading
article to the election. One part of the article which
we are about to quote, and which we might have written
ourselves, we commeund to the careful consideration of
our readers. Here it is:—‘‘ The Unionist who had
‘views’ on this and that subject, on which he failed
to get satisfaction from Mr. Long, may have threatencd
{o vote for the Nationalist, or to stay away. But when
it came to the polling day the waverers must in most
cases have thought better of it, for there is little
evidence of abstention or cross-voting in a total poll of
9,195 votes out of 10,547 on the register, or in Mr.
Long’s own poll of 5,269.”

In the course of an editorial article on the LEADER,
our contemporary, 7'%e People’s Advocate, says:—

One of the most fearless, outspoken and logically-written periodi
cals that we know of is THE LEADER, Dublin.  Though establi:
only six years, it can now claim to be foremost amongst
weekly reviews. A good many papers, recently established, have
tried to imitate THE LEADER'S ““ bill of fare’’; but to the intelligent
observant man, there was a palpable something about their general
“get up” that showed a lamentable lack of sincerity—and hence
their failure. But there is certainly no dishonesty of purpose
about THE LEADzR, or its able editor, Mr. D. P. Moran. It is safe
to azsume—and most of our readers know it—that he has done more
good for the furtherance of the Irish Ireland movement in this
country and across-Channel than most of our present-day patriots.
Indsed, he might be termed as the pioneer of the movement; and
not only did he “start the ship,”” but he ensured that it would not
hit against any rock on its course. The present satisfactory position
due to the stimulating artic
d in THE LEsDER.

that have from time to time appea:

1f we had a few more papers like it —if, for instance, the Dublin
daily press was as consistent and as fearless to express the truth,
then incalculable good would be be done for the nation; and thero
would, of necossity, be a change in the hypocritical modus operandi
of some of our namby-pamby politicians.  Ti LEADER discuscos
in a philosophical and original vein tho topical Irish matters of
the week, and contains several well written articles from the fore-
most writers of the day. Altogether it is well worth buying, well
worth reading, and well worth digesting.

Two “ Idolatrous’’ Dublin shop assistants wrote to
us complaining that a customer of the shop in which
they are employed insisted, though knowing that they
were ‘‘ Idolators,” in thrusting sectarian ‘‘tracts’’
upon them. One of the assistants at the time was
wearing the Sacred Thirst Total Abstinence badge, and
the other was wearing the Anti-Treating badge. The
““tract’’ distributor announced that he would call
again and give the two ‘‘ Idolators” amny instruction
they required; and, though he was informed that he
need not trouble himself in the matter, he did ‘call
again. Now, what would, say, those directors of the
Great Sick and Sourface Railway, who object to intro-
ducing religion into business—when it takes the form
of protesting against their one-sided and sectarian
management—say, if some ‘‘ Idolatrous’’ customer of
the railway intruded himself with tracts upon Brother
Goulding, Napoleon Dent, Percy Bernard, Smitheen
and othor assistants of the railway, tho ordinary stock

~ of which has so considerably deteriorated in value?

We did not read anything from Capt. Shawe-Taylor
uring the South Dublin contest. Now that it is over
W e that a letter from him appears in Monday’s

THE LEADER. 391

Freeman. The result of that election has thrown a
somewhat comic light on that body that call, or miscall,
themselves ‘“ The Reform Association.” Here is onc
paragraph from the letter:— With regard to per-
sonnel, its members occupy a unique position entailing
tho highest responsibility. They include within their
ranks representatives of a class which has till recently
taken little part in public affairs, and who are now
returning determined to do their share in the national
regeneration of their country. They seek to import a
higher, broader, nobler spirit into Irish public life.”’
This in its way is a gem of unconscious comedy. 1In so
far as the so-called ““ Reform Association ’’ is composed
of landlords, it may be true that it consists of a class
““which has till recently taken little part in public
affairs.””  They were probably too busy in collecting
vack-rents and living on this poor country of which they
have been such an unmitigated curse. They are still
taking the money—and the bonus.

We believe Captain Shawe-Taylor means well, and
perhaps it is more his misfortune than his fault that his
being a scion of the landlord anti-Irish gang puffs him
up in class superiority. Having done nothing but bleed
the country of “rents,”” some of them are now “ deter-
mined to do their share in the national regeneration of
the country.”” Well it was time they thought of it.
And what do you think they wish to do? To fall in
behind those who have borne the heat and burden of
the national efforts? Nothing so common as that for our
superior “converts.”” No; in the humble words of
Captain Shawe-Taylor these rent-gatherers “ seek to im-
port a higher, broader, nobler spirit into Irish publ
life.””  We are in two minds whether to laugh at this,
or treat it with becoming indignation ; better laugh at
it, perhaps. And where, pray, did even such an exalted
person—a man of the landlords as distinct from us
poor, grovelling hinds of the mere people—where did he
and his kind get a mandate to “import a higher,
broader, nobler spirit into Irish public life?”’  If the
Captain sends a few more letters like that to the papers,
we may be tempted to write a comedy, and make him
the central comic figure of it. We wonder what does
Captain Shawe-Taylor now think of his friend, or quon-
dam friend, Sloan, M.P., the rat?

An ““ Idolator "’ in the service of the Great Sourface
Railway, at Kingsbridge, recently died. We hear that
as a consequence of the filling of the vacancy created
that seven moves resulted. We further hear that of
the seven, five were ‘‘saved’’ and two were ‘ Idola-
tors,” and that five ‘“saved’’ got increases on pro-
motion, but that the two who happened to be ‘‘ Idola-
tors ” did not get increases.

A new Temperance Hall was opened at Scariff, in the
County Clare, on Sunday, January 21st. The old
school rooms have been turned into premises that will
be available not only for educational purposes at night,
and for technical education, but also as reading and tem-
perance rooms. The Parish Priest, Father .Halpm,
whose paper on' Temperance at the last Catholic Truth
Conference many of our readers will remember, took the
chair, and his speech on the occasion was of Irish Ireland
all conipact. There was a spirit,”” said Father Halpin
in the course of his address, “ abroad that was new—a
spirit of self-reliance and self-respect that bid them
look back to an Irvish Ireland in the past, with its lan-
guage and traditions, and asked them to create an Irish
Treland of the future not unworthy of such descent; and
he would say in one word that in all they might attempt
in their now town hall, in the books -and papers to be
read, and in the plays they might undertake—for he
understood that they had already a dramatic class-—
they should foster this spirit, and aim at such 1dea1§.” 4
He expressly recommended to his audience the reading
of thoe Leaper. ‘It had done,’”’ said he, ‘‘ one great
thing for them in making thousands of Irishmen think
as they had not done before.””

The fourth lecture in Father Selley’s Course of Popu-
lar Astronomy will be delivered on Monday night next

892

(6th inst.) in the Coffey Palace, Townsend St. In the
coming lecture, the condensed syllabus of which we pub-
lish in another column, Father Selley will treat of the
Planet Mars, and any of our readers who cannot, right
off the reel, answer the question, “ Is Mars inhabited 1"’
should not fail to hear what Father Selley has got to
say on that interesting problem. The prices of admis-
sion to the lecture, which commeunces at 8 p.m., are as
usual—6d., 3d., and 2d.

The Drapers’ Brauch of the Gaelic League, Cpaot
na nGaowigteone, 48 North Great George's Street,
Dublin, have issued a circular letter to travellers, manu-
facturers, and others interested in the drapery trade in
ireland, appealing to those connected with the trade to
become members of the branch. We hope there will be
a hearty response to the circular.

We have received a copy of the February Number
of our now popular penny monthly contemporary, the
Catholic Young Man, for short called the C.Y.Af. - Mr.
J. J. Horgan continues his interesting series, “ Catholic
Forces.”” His subject this month is Mr. Michael Sulli-
van, whose name is familiar to our readers, and the
sound of whose calm and gentle voice at Kingsbridge
acts as an irritant on the nerves of Brother Goulding.
Mr. Horgan’s interesting article is illustrated by a full-
page portrait of his subject.

Dear SIR,—In his last letter to the LEapeEr Dr.
Ifenry quotes certain favourable remarks of mine re-
garding his Irish Handbeok. Those remarks are quite
true. In them I gave the impression made on my mind
by Dr. Henry’s work. In my remarks regarding his
“Booklet "’ T just did the same. It is far more difficult
to write a good infants’ book than to write a book for
an advanced class of children. The younger the infants
are, the more difficult is the work of producing a book
suitable for them. It would take too much space to
enter upon the reasons why. I think there are not
many who will not admit the fact. The work entitled
Ceac¢ta Deassa Taevilge is the best Irish primer I
have seen as yet. .

We have had recently an inundation of phrase-books.
T have had occasion to say favourable things about a lot
of them. The favourable things I said were true.
Where there were faults to be corrected, and where I
got the opportunity of doing so, I corrected them pri-
vately.

PeTER O’LEARY, P.P.

«ALL CREEDS, ALL CLASSES” AT SCOUTH
DYUBLIN.}
HE South Dublin Election is in one sense a mere
drop in the ocean. The loss of one seat in
view of the very small amount of power which the Irish
Parliamentary Party, as a whole, are capable of wield-
ing on the trend of Irish events is a mere bagatelle.
In the present political situation of the three kingdoms
it is most desirable to have an active and alert body
of Nationalists ‘“ on the floor of the House,”” and we
are sorry that at least twenty of the new type of Irish-
man created by the Irish Revival are not represented
in the Party in place of, say, twenty crusted ‘‘no far
distant date ’’ men whose eviction from the Party would
be a healthy riddance. We are sorry that an excellent
type of Irish Irelander like Mr. Hazleton is not amongst
the Party. A little Irish Ircland leaven is badly
wanted in that same Party. IHowever, though we re-
cognise the desirability of a Party at present in the
British House of Commons, the great battles of Ireland
must be fought out on Irish soil; and there are battles
to be fought and won here at every side—to mention
only one thing that Irishmen, and Irishmen only, can
do, wo trust that those specially concerned will relieve
Treland of the stigma that she is not able to insure her-
self by the speedy ostablishment of at least one great
Trish Insurance Company.

The result of the South Dublin election is that a
unit less represents Treland in the British Parliament.
And that fact in the National perspective is after all
only a tiny fact. But the election has another and
greater significanco.  The election shows that Eng-
land’s Faithful Garrison did not change its spots. M.
Al Tindlater, who does a popular trade in groceries and
gpirituous refreshments, publicly took counsel with his
own heart; one Talbot Crosbie, a Devolutionist, what-
over that is, wrote a few letters to the papers; a Mr.
Lindsay Crawford, ‘“ grand master *’ of a species of the

animal known as ‘Orangemen * and editor of a bitterly
sectarian and anti-Catholic paper, held meetings and
added some’ extra gaiety to the drab lives of several
youths; but the main fact is that the refuse of South
Bristol—the late Chief Secretary to the Orangemen—
was supported by 5,269 stalwarts of England’s Faithful
Garrison in South Dublint We suppose Mr. William
O’Brien will still go on mumbling about Orange and
Green !

We see that Mr. William Redmond, in face of the
kick from 5,269 of England’s Faithful Garrison in
South Dublin, declared in a speech after the result had
been made known :—‘‘ Some said this was a fight about
religion. It was no such thing (cheers). All we want
is fair play for all. We want no ascendancy of Pro-
testant or Catholic (cheers).”” Now, why this perpetual
cry from weak-kneed “ Idolators’’? Were the Papists
ever intolerant notwithstanding the persecution of the
Catholics by the British Protestants in Ireland? In
face of the fact that we “ Idolators ’’ do not want any
ascendancy, what is the meaning of those protestations?
If Mr. William Redmond and his kind were really
earnest in saying that they wanted no ascendancy of
Protestant or Catholic they would buckle on their
armour with us and fight the glaring Protestant
ascendancy that exists in Ireland to-day, and would
join the Catholic Defence Society. But, no; a lot of
those ““‘no far distant date’’ prophets are so much in
dread of being called ‘‘ bigots’’ by bogus Rathmines
lodgers that they are afraid to fight like men for Irish
Catholics secular and civic rights. But Mr. Willie
Redmond takes a weak delight, even after the knock

down blow from the Protestant vote in South Dublin,
in praising up such a man as one Crawford, one of
whose complaints against Balfour’s government was the
passing of the recent Education Act. Mr. Willie Red-
mond said: —‘“T am glad that this election has shown
that there are plenty of liberal-minded Protestants—
men like Mr. Crosbie and Mr. Crawford and Mr.
Findlater (cheers).”” A man like Crawford, a militant
Protestant, is patted on the back by Willie Redmond,
a ‘“tolerance proving’’ Papist. This is the sort of
namby-pamby spirit that keeps Irish ‘‘Idolators’’
where they are—under the heel of Sourface ascendancy.
Mr. Willie Redmond, in the best style of ‘“no far
distant date ’’ raimeis, declared: —‘‘ It has shown that
the day is soon coming when, with God’s help, we will
all be united for freedom and fair play in Ireland
(cheers).”” Perhaps, so complaisant was the humour of
Mr. Willie Redmond after the knock-down blow from
5,269 of England’s Faithful Garrison that if a voice
shouted ‘‘ three cheers for the man Coote,”” Mr. Willie
would have led off the cheers! Frankly we lose patience
with men—or boys—like Mr. Willie Redmond, who
have been ‘“ no far distant dating ’’ for years and years,
and ‘“ fighting the battle of Ireland on the floor of the
British House of Commons”’ to little purpose.

One of Mr. Al Findlater’s carts passing as Mr.
William Redmond was speaking, Mr. Willie, filled with
brotherly love after the kick from 5,269 votes from
England’s Faithful Garrison in South Dublin, ex-
claimed: ‘“ You might as well give a cheer for old
Findlater now,”” and according to Zink the poor dupes
to whom he was speaking emitted ‘‘ loud cheers.”” No
wonder the weak ‘“ Idolators,”” who are afraid to stand
up like men for their secular and civic rights, and who
fear to face the terrible cannon hall “bigot’’ from the
mouths of the awe-inspiring Rathmines bogus lodgers,
are where they are.

Now Mr. Willie Redmond and others of his ““ toler-
2nce proving ’’ class may go on until doomsday shouting
“* All creeds, all classes,”” and probably they will do so;
but England’s Faithful Garrison will go on chuckling
also, and giving Nationalist candidates in South Dublin
such doses as 5,269 votes between their ‘‘ Idolatrous’”
cyes. A Mr. Al Findlater may wobble, and a Mr.
Stephen Gwynn may go over to the Irish side; but a
wobbler counts for little, and one swallow does not
make a summer. Mr. Willie Redmond, who has words
of praise for the editor of a bigoted and sectarian paper,
the ‘‘ Grand Master”’ of the so-called ““ Independent

COLLEGES ?
ROM the days of my youth ‘upwavrd‘s I havc_been
constantly reading hard things, said or written,
about tho Queen’s Colleges. I am not going to defend
theso unlucky institutions, but merely to survey some
facts of their, present condition. I have always heard
them called “ godless colleges ' by our own side, Wl]llle
those of the other side, having their minds inflated with
the famous “ prestige” of Trinity, never say a word
about them. Asa youngster I used to imagine that be-
cause they wero called ““godless colleges,”” and because
Cardinal Cullen was very much down on f1em, no Catho-
lic ever went to them, or at all events extremely few.
T understood it to be a part of the policy of the Catholic
Hierarchy to close them, if possible, and that to this
end they laid them under some sort of ban or inferdict.
Certainly the old “ Queen’s > University was brought to
the ground, and the “ Royal ’’ established in its stead.
So far the bishops were successful. Meanwhile the old
Catholic University died too, and was “ quietly in-
urned.” So far it was “evens’’ between the bishops
and the Government, though I fancy the inherent ab-
surdity and unfitness of the Queen’s University scheme
had nearly as much to do with making that ill-laid en-
terprise “ gang agley *’ as anything the bishops did. But -
let us turn to the present time.

For a whole course of years after the establishment of

the “ Royal,” the attendance at the Queen’s Colleges, -
after various fluctuations, took a distinct downward dip,
falling from 846 in 1891-92 to 627 in 1900-01. Now,
however, another change seems to have come over mat-
ters, and it appears that last year the number in
attendance rose to 752. Certainly for three colleges of
University grade, 752 is not a flourishing number of
students; still, it is 125 better than four years ago, and
seems to show that the Queen’s Colleges are holding on.
As T have already remarked, I used to imagine that no
Catholics, or almost none, went to the Queen’s Colleges.

best illustrate the wisdom of my assumption : —

;Bul‘., indeed, we have got into such a habit of denounc-

cerities to a degree that might be amusing were the

I am, of course, quite aware that the majority of these
Catholic students belong to the Medical Schools of their
Colleges, not to the Arts’ Course.  Yet this in no way
alters the fact that we could close the majority of the
Queen’s Colleges if we wanted to—aud don’t. If, then,
it is by the attendance of Catholic students that two of
the three Queen’s Colleges are kept alive, thereisno use
pretending that all the denunciation of the Colleges has
been very effective. The recent rise in the number at-
tending the Colleges, too, is another sign that they have
still to be counted with as items in the Irish educational
world. Last year. for instance, 75 Catholics entered
the Queen’s Colleges—10 in Belfast, 54 in Cork, and 11
in Galway. The total uumber of Episcopalians entering
was 60, and of Presbyterians, 89. We see, then, that
the Catholic numbers are keeping up, so that the “ god-
less Colleges ’* are not likely to fail through our people
not furnishing their accustomed quota. In view of all
this it is evident that the ban is not very much use;
the question is—what should be done, or should any-
thing be done?

Personally, I certainly think something could, and
shauld, be done. There is something like £18,000 a
year running nearly to waste in these two Colleges of
Cork and Galway; why not try and make the money
useful? Galway, I think, may be given up. Con-
naught is not in a condition at present, I fear, to keep
a University College going. ~ With Cork, the case is
different. Cork City has an individuality of its own,
and so has the part of Munster which it drains. The
Corkmen have a conceit of themselves; they have even
a certain healthy bumptiousness—witness their backing
of William O’Brien—which bespeaks something of an
independent life. Why should not Cork have a high-
grade University College of its own, and why might not
Queen’s College be made that institution? If anyone
asks how, I should commend him to Dr. Delany’s
“TLetter ”’ on the University question. There he will
find out how the Presbyterians managed to capture the
Belfast College for themselves. When the Colleges were
about to be founded, one of the lay leaders of the North-
eastern Presbyterians wrote to Sir Robert Peel, the Pre-
mizr in 1845, informing him that “the presence of a

single Roman Catholic *” on the staff of the Arts’ Course
would prevent the Preshyterians, for whom the Belfast
College was chiefly intended, from availing of it. Ac-
cordingly, when the College was opened, there were no
(Catholics on that staff, nor has therc cver been “a
single Roman Catholic’ on it since. Here, then, are
lines to work upon. Let the Bishops arrange a quiet
bargain to the effect that if the stafl of the Arts’ Course
in the Cork College is made acceptable—that Catholics
shall fill the chairs of logic, metaphysics, philosophy, ete.
.—that then Cork College shall be freed from any ban on
the part of their lordships: It is impossib}e for the Go-
“vernment to place any tangible obstacle in the way of
such an arrangement. They have all'_eady put a parallel
one in force at Belfast for Presbyterian convenience, so
~ that they could net justly refuse to dp likewise at Cork
for Catholics. There is surely Catholic material enough

two principles to be adopted in any division of Ireland

migrants should be left to govern themselves , or to be

with permission, of course, to throw in their lot with

real Irish—which in Ulster practically means Catholics

AN IRISH-AMERICAN OM IRELAND.

Dear Sin—Nothing in the line of news or literature
to Irvish-Americans at this side of the Atlantic is so
welcome as the Leapsr. It is so different from the old-
fashioned Irish organs inasmuch as it gives expression
to the truth, whether it pleases its friends or enemies,
and strikes at the root of many abuses which the Press
in the past was too timid to touch, fearing, I suppose,
it would cost them the loss of a few advertisers or
subscribers.  This, to my.mind, has also been the
fault of many of our public men who filled us with the
ideas that we were the best people on earth, and when
we wake up in foreign lands we find out our mistake
and wish they, like the LeADER, had told us some of our
weaknesses and helped us to correct them.

I was born in Ireland and visit there as often as I can
spare time, as I love every inch of it. Last year I spent
from May to September in various parts of the Island,
and was specially favoured with beautiful weather,
which I was told was one of the finest seasons they had
for years. Strange, the keepers of various resorts were
astonished at the scarcity of patrons and tourists, but
1, like your able correspondent (whose article of
October, the 7th, in the LEADER, on fewness of tourists
to Killarney, and the reasons), was not a bit surprised.

Your correspondent told of the dirt of Killarney’s
streets and its lack of public attractions at evening for
visitors. That is true of all places of interest or health
resorts in Irveland, from Antrim to Cork.

Nature has done wonders for the country, but the
inhabitants very little ; they never think at any of these
resorts that both the young and old at vacation time
like a bit of fun to pass away the time; they never
exert themselves getting together to build a hall for
concerts or dancing to help to pass away the long hours *
of evening at watering places. ~Many of the shop-
keepers in these places are well fixed and have money
in bank, bub you could not make them see by bringing
patrons in larger numbers to places would be much
better for them than the little interest they get for their
savings in a bank.

T spent some time at a watering place in the South of
Ireland, called Ballybunion, Co. Kerry, and I venture
to say that a finer strand or bathing place is not in the
Continent. It has picturesque cliffs and caves, and is
well patronised by surrounding counties and has a very
thriving population. It has grown in size, but what
have the inhabitants done to attract people.  Thirty
years ago, when I was a boy, they had a fiddler in the
Castle Green, who afforded some little amusement, but
he died, and they have not since attempted to make

the placo attractive by baths, or concert hall which
could be used for reading or dancing during the long
evenings. At least four or five thousand guests come
there every season, and they have not a third-rate
musical instrument in the town. This is no libel on the
place, as, when a concert was held for the benefit of
the Sisters of Mercy, on the 29th of July, there was
not a single piano in any of the hotels, which could
accompany the various artists; ane had to be borrowed
from the doctor’s wife who lived in the outskirts of the
town. What a torture it was in the twilight to listen
to some of these instruments and some of the local
Paderewskis rasping away. That—and the mournful
music of the sad sea waves—was awful on the nerve.
A witty American said to me it was well the Irish had
such faith, or believed witli Hamlet “ on the dread of
something after death,’” otherwise the coroner would be
kept busy from the number who would ‘ shuffle off
mortal coil” from the inviting cliffs.
|

Your correspondent said the Killarney people were
much disappointed at lack of American tourists. It
becomes more surprising when it is well known at this
side, by figures, that a larger number of American
touvists went te Europe last season than at any other
time in the history of the country’; and it is computed
they spent in Europe sixteen million dollars, or three
million two hundred thousand pounds (£3,200,000).
‘Why has Ireland not got her share of it? For the
simple reason that she does not give the value in accom-
modation or attractions. An American is a methodical
business mau, as a rule, lavish in his habits and expen-
ditures, but expects good treatment, and, I say not as
an enemy of the old land, but a sincere friend, that we
are a hundred years behind the times in hotels and the
things that attract tourists.

You can see those huge Atlantic liners at Queenstown
going to Liverpool from New York, and possibly only
about five per cent. of passengers get off at Queenstown,
and very often half on board are of Irish or Irish
descent, or in some way counected with, and sym-
pathisers with, the land.

T know well that Ireland suffers from not having her
own governmeunt, but people at resorts ought to have
more enterprise, and try to make the places attractive,
at least for their own people. Why blame Americans
for not visiting the places, when your own Catholic
clerzymen, publiic men, professional men, merchants and
their families, and the clerks in cities will not be found
at Irish resorts, but you can find them at Brighton,
Buxton, Harrogate, Guernsey, Isle of Man, and other
British places in numbers. This I can vouch for, as I
passed a day’s visit to most of the places referred, but
was always 1n a hurry to get back to the Emerald Isle.

Now, 1u conclusion, let me assure you that I have
worked at home and here for the land of my birth, and
that whatever I write is for her welfare: but to get
tourists in numbers you must build halls and get up
inexpensive ball alleys, shuffle boards, quoits, tennis
courts, croquet grounds, etc. All these cost little in
proportion to what you lose by not having them, and
will repay you in a couple of years by keeping your own
young people at home. The hotel-keepers should also
be moderate in charges, and give value that would
bring back patrons again. Have regular prices for
drives and excursions in boats, instead of a great many
leaving it to ‘‘ Ye’re Honor,”” and ‘‘ Ye're Honor ’’ al-
ways having to pay double what it should be; and, in
conclusion, to Americans be regular for meal times, and
have bedroom tidy, as it is a country of perfect order
and system.—Yours very faithfully,

Parrick TTocan, M.D.

Lven among the “ Commercials ”” a wave of temper-
ance is apparenfly noticeable.

At a certain Irish hotel, where a genial old “ Larry *’
assists the waitress in handing round the plates, a dozen

Larry, as usual, was busy bustling round, serving the
courses, or running to the bar for drinks for any of the
diners who desired something to wash down the bacon.
At the bar “ the missis,”” in all her pomp, dignity, and
state, presided and waited impatiently for Larry’s
orders.

Whether it was the wet atmosphere, or the “ wave,”’
wo know not, hut orders for drinks were singularly limi-
ted on this occasion, and Larry’s persistent and insinu-
ating reminders to the diners were useless.

“ Will it be a Bass, Mr. 0.2’

“H’'m, no, Larry, nothing to-day.”

“Och, try a bottle, sir; it’s bad to be atin’ and not
drinkin’.”’

But Larry failed to make any impression, and he grew
strangely silent, and when he came back from the bar
with the second and last drink ordered, he gave a long
sustained wink to the waitress.

“ Begor the New Year began well,”” whispered he into
her ear, with sarcastic emphasis on “ well.””

“ What's the matter?’ queried the waitress, as she
scented fun,

“ What’s the matter, is it? Yerra, don’t yih sec what

ing up the forefinger of his left hand to illustrate what
was coming), he added with most awful solemnity—
“Wan bottle of Guinness, and wan bottle of Lager!”—
and joining his hands and looking up to the ceiling, he
murmured, “ Glory be to heavens, she’s raygin’ 11"’

N the Z7ish World of January 20th just to hand there

is a lengthy first notice of the Philosophy of Irish

Ireland. Our readers, we feel sure, will be interested

to read the views set forth in our famous Irish-American
contemporary : —

William B. Yeats, in_onc of his lecturas in thi country, held
Up a volume of Lady Grogory’s and said: “This is ono of the
most remarkable books which have been produced in Iroland; in
fact I think it is the best book which has como ouf of Iroland
in my time.” We borrow the cxpression and leavo tho comparison,
and say that “The Philosophy of Irish Ircland” is in our opinion
the best book which has como out of Treland in our time. It is the
ropublication of six articles which appeared in the New [reland
Review between Decombor. 1898, and August, 1900, by D. P.
Moran, who, the month following the last montioned date, happily
secured an unlimited field for the expression and devclopment of
his views in THE LeADER, which was then founded and of which
he became and continues to bo the editor. Some of the articles wo
had read in their original print, and others we had not the oppor-
tunity of reading until now.

It may be said at the outsei that there is no new discovery put
forth in these articles upon which theorists can philosophize until
another discovery turns up; nor is there any new panacea for
Ireland's ills to be found in them, which amateur statesmon can
use for debate until another panacca is put forward. The book is
the more intensely interesting because of this: It is a masterly
diagn of Ireland as she is. uninfluenced on the one hand by the
catch-cries which have pas:ed so long as watchwords of nationality.
and fearless of the consequences of smashing popular idols; and
unmarred on the other by ihe cold materialism which ignores
racial characteristics developed by centuries, and national sentiment,
however alloyed—a quality which has led not only to failure but
to provocation—the well meant efforts of so many others to im-
prove the condition of Ireland. The book is the production of
a thinker; not a thinker who speaks in academic fashion to
statesmen or cconomists, bui who has keenly analyzed facts of
daily Irish life visible aud familiar to all—-many of them so
familiar that few ever looked at. much less thought about them
—analyzed them in the light of blazing professions of nationality.
and has left all who are concerned with that nationality to judge
the result.

Let it not be supposed from this that the book is given to merc
destructive criticism or carping fault-finding; it is rather intro
spective and analytic than critical, and essentially constructive.
conducive to sound thought, to sound, self-reliant and uncompro-
mising nationality. Destructive it certainly is—of cant, humbug
and shoneenism, and of the nationality spoken from the lips that
is belied by the actions dictated by the heart. None of ““union
of hearts’’ or ‘ non-political and mon-sectarian ”’ humbug of the
patrons of industry or agriculturc to be found in these pages,
although politics as a usurper of nationality is severely castigated
—too severely, many, even Irish Irclanders, will s but the
excess of severity, if any, may be forgiven if the result is to
arouse thought in minds that were heretofore drifting or dreaming.

Many passages could be excerpted from their context in Mr.
Moran’s articles which would bring a storm of howls around his
cars and charges of anti-nationalism at his head, but those who
indulge in this species of argument and adopt this mecans of
“serving ” Ireland are the very ones who most need *“ The Philo-
sophy of Irish freland.” for one of tho chief lessons of the work is
honest thought, honest criticism. ““ Tell the truth though the sky
